title: AI’s next phase is being built in public.
sections: Good morning, Anthony. Today is Wednesday, July fifteenth. Your GoJo is tuned to artificial-intelligence business strategy, the New York Mets, media and streaming, and financial markets. We’ll start with the physical buildout behind AI, then move through the Mets’ trade-deadline decision, a shift in streaming strategy, and the tension between strong corporate profits and higher oil prices.

The artificial-intelligence race is shifting from impressive demonstrations toward the physical systems required to make the technology useful at scale. Chipmakers, cloud providers, and industrial companies are increasing spending on data centers, advanced processors, networking equipment, and the electricity needed to run it all. That spending is one reason investors continue to support the AI trade, even while asking when these enormous investments will translate into durable profits. The key change is that businesses are becoming more selective. They are looking past general-purpose chatbots and asking where AI can improve a specific workflow, reduce labor, or create a product customers will pay for. The winners may be determined less by who has the flashiest model and more by who controls distribution, proprietary data, infrastructure, and a clear customer relationship. Watch for companies to become more explicit about measurable returns—not just the amount they are spending.

With the trade deadline approaching, the Mets are weighing short-term reinforcements against the value of preserving prospects and payroll flexibility. The club’s position is not simply a choice between buying and selling. Management can pursue smaller upgrades that improve the current roster without surrendering the young players it believes could support the next competitive window. The next stretch of games will determine how aggressively the front office acts. A sustained run would strengthen the case for help in the bullpen or another dependable bat. An uneven stretch would make lower-risk moves more likely. The decision matters because it will reveal whether the organization believes this roster can contend now—or whether its longer-term plan still takes priority. Watch the quality of the prospects involved in any rumor; that will say more than the size of the headline.

Major entertainment platforms are reorganizing bundles, sports rights, advertising tiers, and release schedules around a more mature streaming market. The emphasis is moving away from collecting the largest possible number of subscribers and toward keeping valuable households engaged across a broader mix of programming. That makes loyalty more important than raw growth. Live sports can reduce cancellations, lower-priced advertising plans can widen the audience, and bundles can make a service feel harder to give up. The strategic question is whether these combinations create genuine value or simply recreate the expensive cable package that streaming originally replaced. The next competitive advantage may come from controlling distribution and reducing churn, not commissioning more shows. Watch how platforms describe engagement and profit—not just subscriber totals—in their next results.

Stocks are finding support in better-than-expected corporate earnings, but higher oil prices are complicating the outlook. Strong profits suggest many businesses are handling the current economy well. Persistent energy costs, however, could revive inflation pressure and limit how quickly central banks can lower interest rates. Markets can usually absorb an isolated shock. The larger risk is oil feeding into transportation, manufacturing, and household costs at the same time, turning a temporary increase into broader price pressure. That would force investors to reconsider the optimistic assumption that inflation will continue easing while economic growth remains steady. For now, earnings are providing a cushion. Watch whether companies begin warning that fuel and shipping costs are affecting margins; that would show the oil move is spreading beyond commodity markets.

That is your GoJo for this morning: AI spending is moving from models to infrastructure and measurable workflows; the Mets are balancing present help against their longer plan; streaming is prioritizing loyalty over growth at any cost; and markets are weighing strong profits against the inflation risk of expensive oil. Have a good morning.
